reason: Copper elbows use the same compression connection — technicians often need both on a job
Tee fittings branch the line where compression fittings join — used together on copper runs
Pipe clips secure the copper pipe on either side of the compression joint
Isolation valves use compression ends — often installed adjacent to compression fittings
Pipe clips hold the pipe steady near push-fit joints to prevent movement
Reducers transition pipe sizes before a push-fit connection
Ball valves are the modern replacement for gate valves — consider upgrading on older systems
Gate valves often connect via compression fittings on copper pipework
Isolation valves provide local shut-off downstream of the main ball valve
Flexi connectors often attach directly after a ball valve at fixtures
Flexible connectors attach to stop valves under sinks and behind toilets
Isolation valves perform a similar function — technicians often carry both
P-traps and flexi connectors are both needed when replacing a basin or sink
A check valve prevents backflow contamination where the float valve connects to mains supply
Flexi connectors supply water to the float valve in modern cistern installations
Filling loops require a check valve to prevent heating water contaminating the mains
Both are critical safety devices — check valves prevent backflow, PRVs prevent overpressure
Always install an isolation valve upstream of a flexi connector for easy maintenance
Stop valves and flexi connectors work together at every tap and toilet connection
Clips are needed near elbows to support the pipe run at direction changes
Iso valves sit between the supply pipe and the flexi connector at each fixture
Elbows and tees are the two most-used fittings on any copper pipe installation
Pipe clips secure the run near elbow joints to prevent stress on the solder joint
Reducing tees and pipe reducers work together to branch to a smaller pipe size
Tee fittings and reducers are used together when branching to a different pipe diameter
Schrader valves are the service access points used when checking TXV superheat
Service valves are accessed when replacing filter driers on the liquid line
Always replace the filter drier when replacing a TXV — contaminants damage the new valve
Schrader valves are needed to check superheat after TXV installation or adjustment
TXVs are directly downstream of filter driers — both are serviced together
Dampers control airflow distribution from the air handler through the ductwork
The air handler (indoor) and condenser (outdoor) are the two halves of a split system
Dampers are installed in the ductwork connected to the air handler
The condenser and air handler must be matched — service one, check the other
If the expansion vessel fails, the PRV is the last line of defense against overpressure
After replacing an expansion vessel, re-pressurize the system via the filling loop
Both are core sealed-system components — often checked together during boiler service
Circulator pumps and zone valves work together to distribute heat through the system
Both sit on the heating circuit — check one when servicing the other
A discharging PRV often means the expansion vessel has failed — check it first
Overfilling via the filling loop is a common cause of PRV discharge
Zone valves open to allow flow — the circulator pump provides the motive force
TRVs provide room-level control within the zone that the motorized valve serves
The circulator pump pushes water through the heat exchanger — poor flow causes kettling
Both are internal boiler components — expansion vessels protect the heat exchanger from overpressure
Every radiator has a TRV on one end and a lockshield on the other — always paired
TRVs and lockshields are always installed as a pair on each radiator
TRVs handle room-level temperature while zone valves control the zone circuit
Check valves are required on filling loops to prevent heating water backflowing into mains
If you're using the filling loop frequently, the expansion vessel may have failed
Overfilling via the filling loop causes PRV discharge — fill to correct pressure only
